title: Milei and Argentina’s Two-Speed Economy
gist: Argentina under Milei shows a two-speed economy — booming agriculture, oil and lithium alongside falling consumption and investment — with inflation crushed from 211% to 31%, reserves doubled to $49 billion, and country risk collapsing from 1,900 to 400 basis points, but political risk (Kicillof, corruption scandals, 2027 election) still looms large.

insights.claim: Argentina's agriculture sector surged 32.2% year-on-year last December, helping drive overall GDP growth of 4.4% for the year, per INDEC (Argentina's national statistics institute).
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Agricultural strength is doing much of the heavy lifting in headline GDP growth — weather/commodity-price swings in that sector are an outsized macro risk factor.
insights.locator: On the fast track: Agriculture

insights.claim: Argentina's oil output rose 13% last year on the Vaca Muerta shale fields in Neuquén, gas rose 1.7%, mining overall advanced 3.3%, and lithium production soared more than 40% even as gold and silver output declined.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Vaca Muerta and lithium are the clearest structural growth engines in Argentina right now — a concrete sector-level allocation signal.
insights.locator: The other stars are oil, up 13% last year

insights.claim: While export sectors boom, public consumption fell 0.9% annually, investment fell 11.6%, and private surveys show consumer spending dropped 3% between January and May of this year.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Domestic demand and investment remain weak even as export sectors boom — consumer- and investment-facing Argentine businesses face a tougher environment than headline GDP suggests.
insights.locator: In the slow lane: Public consumption declined

insights.claim: Argentina's unemployment fell slightly from 8% to 7.8% over the past year, but roughly 200,000 people shifted from formal private employment to informal jobs, which remain stuck at around 40% of the active population for most of the past two decades.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Headline unemployment improvement masks a formalization problem — informality near 40% is a structural drag on tax revenue and productivity that persists regardless of the cycle.
insights.locator: Unemployment has fallen slightly

insights.claim: Argentina's annual inflation plummeted from 211% in 2023 to 31% in 2025 due to fiscal and monetary tightening, a trend expected to continue this year, though the cost of living remains among the highest in the world.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: A roughly 180-point disinflation in two years is the single clearest macro win of the Milei program, but the cost-of-living overhang caps the political payoff.
insights.locator: Inflation plummeted from 211% in 2023

insights.claim: Private consultants and multilateral organizations forecast Argentine GDP growth of around 3% to 3.6% this year.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: A consensus growth forecast in the 3-3.6% range gives investors a benchmark against which to judge whether the recovery is broadening as Milei needs.
insights.locator: private consultants and multilateral organizations forecast

insights.claim: Argentina moved from an energy deficit of $270 million in 2023 to a surplus of $7.8 billion in 2025, a surplus that could double by the end of this year, becoming more significant amid recent Middle East turmoil.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: The flip to a growing energy trade surplus is a structural improvement to Argentina's external accounts, reducing historic vulnerability to hard-currency shocks.
insights.locator: Argentina has also recently shifted from being an energy importer

insights.claim: Central bank (BCRA) reserves have roughly doubled to about $49 billion from 2023 levels, after the government—under IMF pressure—abandoned its initial stance of not accumulating reserves and surpassed by midyear the $10 billion additional-reserves target set for 2026.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Reserve accumulation ahead of schedule reduces one of Argentina's classic crisis triggers (BCRA reserve shortage) and strengthens the case for capital-market re-access.
insights.locator: the government abandoned its initial stance

insights.claim: Argentine country risk has fallen from 1,900 basis points at the start of Milei's administration to about 400 points currently, potentially enabling Argentina's return to international debt markets for the first time since before the 2018-2019 crisis that ended Mauricio Macri's presidency.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: A drop from 1,900 to 400 bps is a dramatic re-rating that could reopen international bond markets to Argentina — a concrete catalyst to watch for a sovereign issuance.
insights.locator: Argentine country risk has fallen

insights.claim: During the transition, Milei financed the government via local-market issuance and international bank loans backed by World Bank and IADB collateral, rather than foreign-law bond sales.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: The reliance on collateralized multilateral-backed loans instead of open-market bonds shows Argentina's credit access is still not fully normalized despite falling country risk.
insights.locator: In the meantime, Milei opted for issuing in the local market

insights.claim: Tax revenues have declined in real terms over the past nine months, forcing deeper spending cuts to meet the annual fiscal surplus target of 1.4% of GDP pledged to the IMF.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Falling real tax revenue means the fiscal-surplus commitment is being met through austerity rather than growth — a fragile equilibrium if revenue keeps declining.
insights.locator: Tax revenues have declined in real terms

insights.claim: The government increased social spending for the poorest households above the 33% annual inflation rate even while cutting public-sector wages, pensions, and infrastructure spending, and the 2025 poverty rate fell to 28%, the lowest in seven years.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Targeted social spending amid broader austerity is a deliberate political-risk hedge — a pragmatic carve-out investors should note when assessing Milei's willingness to compromise.
insights.locator: Milei's inner circle is betting on a supply-side stimulus

insights.claim: Real wages remain below the levels at the start of Milei's administration, largely due to the unfreezing of public service tariffs and transport fares that had been held down under Alberto Fernández, creating a contradiction between falling inflation and falling disposable income for middle- and lower-middle-class households.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: The gap between headline disinflation and household purchasing power is the key political vulnerability — it explains why Milei's popularity has not fully benefited from the inflation win.
insights.locator: Real wages are below the levels at the start

insights.claim: Argentine exports reached $40.4 billion between January and May of this year, up 24.3% versus the same period in 2025, driven by higher volumes (+15.7%) and better international prices (+7.5%).
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Strong export growth from both volume and price confirms the commodity/energy sectors are genuinely expanding output, not just riding price effects.
insights.locator: Between January and May of this year, Argentine exports

insights.claim: Foreign direct investment has been falling for a year despite the export boom, as foreign companies demand unrestricted capital outflows and remain wary of Argentina's deep political divide between libertarians and kirchneristas.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: FDI hesitancy despite booming exports signals capital controls and political-risk perception remain the binding constraint on foreign investment, not the underlying growth story.
insights.locator: By contrast, foreign direct investment has been falling

insights.claim: The author's core thesis is that if Argentina's economic recovery broadens and reaches households more tangibly, Milei could rebuild political capital ahead of next year's presidential election; if it doesn't, his popularity — already hurt by corruption allegations — stays under pressure.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: The gap between macro data and lived experience is the single variable most likely to determine Milei's 2027 electoral prospects — watch household-level consumption and real-wage data over GDP headlines.
insights.locator: Yet many Argentines are still not feeling the benefits

insights.claim: Last year's midterm elections, in which leftist governor Axel Kicillof won the Buenos Aires provincial race, triggered a financial crisis that was only contained through direct intervention by the U.S. Treasury at Donald Trump's direction.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Argentina's market stability has already proven dependent on explicit U.S. Treasury backstopping during political shocks — a real tail-risk mitigant, but also a dependency to flag.
insights.locator: leftist governor Axel Kicillof's victory

insights.claim: If Axel Kicillof becomes the main opposition candidate for president next year, Milei's reelection prospects may improve, since independent voters could reject a figure closely tied to the Cristina Fernández de Kirchner/Alberto Fernández era.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: A Kicillof candidacy is framed as favorable for Milei's reelection odds — a specific electoral matchup worth tracking as a market catalyst.
insights.locator: If Kicillof emerges as the main opposition candidate

insights.claim: Milei's chief of staff Manuel Adorni resigned over illicit-enrichment allegations (which he denies) and was replaced by Diego Santilli, a figure from former President Macri's camp considered politically shrewd; polls suggest these corruption allegations remain secondary to voters more focused on income and employment.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: Corruption scandals have so far not moved the political needle versus bread-and-butter economic concerns — a signal that economic data, not scandal news flow, will drive Milei's approval.
insights.locator: recent corruption scandals involving government officials

insights.claim: The author frames Milei's approach as an 'all-or-nothing gamble' to break Argentina's inflation curse — entrenched since the 1970s and already tamed elsewhere in Latin America except Venezuela — through investor-confidence-building via sweeping deregulation, against a history of ten sovereign defaults and Macri's earlier collapse.
insights.speaker: Martín Kanenguiser
insights.so_what: The author explicitly frames Milei's program as high-variance rather than a sure thing — a reminder to size any Argentina bet with historical default risk in mind.
insights.quote: he will continue to play an all-or-nothing gamble
insights.locator: The official recipe is clear
